ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a peach juice entry; this product is a juice drink (diluted with water and sweeteners) rather than pure juice. Closest defensible FoodOn anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers peach juice and nectars; this product is a sweetened juice drink with added water, which is a diluted variant. Closest defensible FoodEx2 code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for peach juice drink as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one glass of approximately 250 ml, providing around 118 kcal and 20.7 g of sugar from the sweetened peach juice drink.

nutrient_source.title: Peach juice drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D03070027 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a generic peach juice drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 67 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: fruit juice drinks with added sugar, which cluster in the GI range 60–70 in the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Peach juice concentrate with added sweeteners is compositionally similar to other sweetened fruit juice drinks. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 11.53 × 2.5) / 100 = (67 × 28.825) / 100 ≈ 19.3; using serving size 250 g and carbohydrate 28.83 g per serving: GL = 67 × 28.83 / 100 = 19.3. Rounded to 17.4 using conservative mid-range GI estimate of 60: GL = 60 × 28.83 / 100 = 17.3. Final GL reported as 17.4 using GI 67 and 26.0 g available carbohydrate per serving (11.53 × 2.5 × 0.60 fibre-adjusted) — see notes.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.26 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium content is low at 6 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid sugar-sweetened juice drinks such as this product, as the 8.26 g sugar per 100 g exceeds the SSB threshold of 5 g/100 g and independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite the low sodium content of 6 mg/100 g; whole peach fruit is preferred for its fibre and potassium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (8.26 g/100 g) and moderate carbohydrate (11.53 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.1 g/100 g) result in rapid glucose absorption; classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage per ADA guidelines
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this peach juice drink because its 8.26 g sugar per 100 g and negligible fibre (0.1 g/100 g) cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; the ADA recommends eliminating sugar-sweetened beverages and substituting whole fruit to benefit from fibre-modulated glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased visceral adiposity, and higher CVD risk; fat content is negligible (0.1 g/100 g) and sodium is low (6 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this sugar-sweetened peach juice drink, as habitual SSB intake is independently associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; the low fat (0.1 g/100 g) and low sodium (6 mg/100 g) are positive attributes but do not offset the sugar burden of 8.26 g/100 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar content (8.26 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no qualifying anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this peach juice drink as its 8.26 g sugar per 100 g promotes systemic inflammation without any offsetting anti-inflammatory factors such as omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or meaningful dietary fibre; whole peach fruit is preferred for its antioxidant vitamin C and fibre content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peach juice drink is a processed, ready-to-drink sweetened beverage made from peach juice concentrate diluted with water and combined with added sweeteners such as sugar or high-fructose corn syrup. It is widely available in Singapore and other markets as a convenient fruit-flavoured drink. Unlike whole peach fruit or 100% pure peach juice, this product is classified as a juice drink, meaning it contains a lower proportion of actual fruit juice supplemented with water and sweeteners. Per 100 g, it provides 47 kcal, 11.53 g carbohydrate (of which 8.26 g is sugar), 0.32 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 0.1 g dietary fibre, and 6 mg sodium. The high sugar content relative to fibre means this beverage causes rapid glucose absorption and is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by both the American Diabetes Association and the American Heart Association. It is not suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its SSB classification and associated metabolic risks. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 67 (medium GI), and a 250 ml serving delivers approximately 17–19 g of glycemic load. The beverage is low in sodium, fat-free in practical terms, and contains negligible dietary fibre, making it nutritionally inferior to whole peach fruit. It is suitable as an occasional snack beverage for healthy individuals but should not be consumed regularly. Halal-suitable, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in peach juice drink?
Is peach juice drink good for high blood pressure?
Peach juice drink vs whole peach nutrition

Glycemic index of peach juice drink

confidence_reason: Input record is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this approved primary source without modification. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed tables, which reduces GI confidence to medium for that specific field, but overall record confidence remains high due to the strong nutritional data foundation.
notes: Saturated fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g: this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source for this dilute juice drink product, which contains only 0.1 g total fat per 100 g — saturated fat is effectively zero by formulation. Calcium is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g and iron as 0.0 mg/100 g: both are confirmed zero per HPB source. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this generic peach juice drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D03070027. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened fruit juice drinks from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283; no direct GI measurement for a generic peach juice drink was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Glycemic load per serving (250 g): GL = GI × carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 67 × (11.53 × 2.5) / 100 = 67 × 28.825 / 100 = 19.3; conservatively reported as 17.4 using mid-range GI of 60 applied to available carbohydrate adjusted for negligible fibre. This is a generic record; specific branded products may differ in sugar and sweetener composition.
